data.sourcePassage: The Black Death was a severe outbreak of bubonic plague that affected England from 1348 to 1350. The disease had already spread across parts of Asia, the Middle East and Europe before it reached England. According to the revision guide, it first arrived at Melcombe Regis in Dorset in June 1348, probably through ships carrying infected rats and fleas. From ports, the disease moved rapidly across the country. Bristol was hit in August 1348 and London in September 1348. The outbreak was devastating because it killed people quickly and spread through towns, villages and trade routes before people understood the real cause.
data.targetPassage: The Black Death was a severe outbreak of bubonic plague that affected England from 1348 to 1350. The disease had already spread across parts of Asia, the Middle East and Europe before it reached England. According to the revision guide, it first arrived at Melcombe Regis in Dorset in June 1348, probably through ships carrying infected rats and fleas. From ports, the disease moved rapidly across the country. Bristol was hit in August 1348 and London in September 1348. The outbreak was devastating because it killed people quickly and spread through towns, villages and trade routes before people understood the real cause.

questions.question: When did the Black Death affect England?
questions.modelAnswer: From 1348 to 1350.

questions.question: Where did it first arrive in England?
questions.modelAnswer: At Melcombe Regis in Dorset.

questions.question: Why did ports help the disease spread?
questions.modelAnswer: Ships could carry infected rats and fleas into port towns, allowing the disease to move inland.

questions.question: Which city was reached in September 1348?
questions.modelAnswer: London was reached in September 1348.

data.sourcePassage: The real cause of the Black Death was bubonic plague. The disease was not spread simply by rats themselves, but by infected fleas that lived on rats. When rats travelled on ships and through dirty towns, their fleas could bite humans and pass on the infection. Medieval streets were often crowded and unhygienic. Sewage and waste could flow in the streets, and people did not have modern toilets, rubbish collection or knowledge of bacteria. This made it easier for disease to spread quickly. However, people at the time did not understand germs or infection, so many explanations were based on religion, astrology or bad smells rather than science.
data.targetPassage: The real cause of the Black Death was bubonic plague. The disease was not spread simply by rats themselves, but by infected fleas that lived on rats. When rats travelled on ships and through dirty towns, their fleas could bite humans and pass on the infection. Medieval streets were often crowded and unhygienic. Sewage and waste could flow in the streets, and people did not have modern toilets, rubbish collection or knowledge of bacteria. This made it easier for disease to spread quickly. However, people at the time did not understand germs or infection, so many explanations were based on religion, astrology or bad smells rather than science.

questions.question: What was the real disease behind the Black Death?
questions.modelAnswer: Bubonic plague.

questions.question: What actually spread the infection from rats to humans?
questions.modelAnswer: Infected fleas spread it by biting humans.

questions.question: How did poor hygiene help the disease spread?
questions.modelAnswer: Crowded dirty streets, sewage and waste made conditions in which rats, fleas and disease could spread more easily.

questions.question: Why did medieval people often give incorrect explanations?
questions.modelAnswer: They did not understand germs, bacteria or infection.

data.sourcePassage: People learned to identify the Black Death through observation. At first, victims suffered flu-like symptoms such as sweating, coughing and a high temperature. Then large lumps appeared on the body, usually on the arms and legs. These swellings were called buboes. They were first red and full of blood, but later turned black as blood dried out in the body. Blotches could also appear over the skin. The disease was terrifying because it was painful, visible and usually fatal. The revision guide says that most people who caught the Black Death died within five days.
data.targetPassage: People learned to identify the Black Death through observation. At first, victims suffered flu-like symptoms such as sweating, coughing and a high temperature. Then large lumps appeared on the body, usually on the arms and legs. These swellings were called buboes. They were first red and full of blood, but later turned black as blood dried out in the body. Blotches could also appear over the skin. The disease was terrifying because it was painful, visible and usually fatal. The revision guide says that most people who caught the Black Death died within five days.

questions.question: Name two early symptoms of the Black Death.
questions.modelAnswer: Sweating, coughing and high temperature are valid examples.

questions.question: What were buboes?
questions.modelAnswer: Large painful swellings or lumps on the body.

questions.question: Where did buboes usually appear?
questions.modelAnswer: Usually on the arms and legs.

questions.question: Why did the disease seem especially frightening?
questions.modelAnswer: It spread quickly, caused visible painful symptoms and most victims died within days.

questions.question: How long did most victims survive after catching it?
questions.modelAnswer: Most died within five days.

data.sourcePassage: Medieval people did not know that fleas and bacteria caused the plague. Many believed the Black Death was a punishment from God, so they prayed more or punished themselves to show repentance. Some people, called flagellants, whipped themselves in public. Others believed the disease came from bad air, known as miasma, or from an unusual alignment of planets. Because doctors did not understand the real cause, treatments were ineffective. People carried sweet-smelling herbs or flowers, used dried toads and leeches, placed figs on buboes, or used butter to soften swollen lumps. These actions show how medieval medicine mixed observation, religion and superstition.
data.targetPassage: Medieval people did not know that fleas and bacteria caused the plague. Many believed the Black Death was a punishment from God, so they prayed more or punished themselves to show repentance. Some people, called flagellants, whipped themselves in public. Others believed the disease came from bad air, known as miasma, or from an unusual alignment of planets. Because doctors did not understand the real cause, treatments were ineffective. People carried sweet-smelling herbs or flowers, used dried toads and leeches, placed figs on buboes, or used butter to soften swollen lumps. These actions show how medieval medicine mixed observation, religion and superstition.

questions.question: What did many people believe God had done?
questions.modelAnswer: They believed God had sent the plague as a punishment.

questions.question: What were flagellants?
questions.modelAnswer: People who whipped themselves as punishment to please God.

questions.question: What does miasma mean?
questions.modelAnswer: The belief that bad air caused disease.

questions.question: Why were treatments ineffective?
questions.modelAnswer: They did not deal with the real cause: infected fleas and plague bacteria.

questions.question: Which treatment was used to soften lumps?
questions.modelAnswer: Butter was used to soften swollen lumps.

data.sourcePassage: The Black Death changed England immediately. Whole villages and towns were wiped out, and England’s population fell by about one-third in only two years. Some people left towns for the countryside to avoid infection. Fear also led to persecution: foreigners and non-Christians, including Jews, were wrongly blamed for causing the disease, and some were attacked or forced into separate parts of towns. The economy also changed. With fewer peasants, agricultural land was left unused and livestock died because no one cared for them. Food supplies fell, prices rose, and surviving peasants and craftsmen could demand higher wages because workers had become scarce.
data.targetPassage: The Black Death changed England immediately. Whole villages and towns were wiped out, and England’s population fell by about one-third in only two years. Some people left towns for the countryside to avoid infection. Fear also led to persecution: foreigners and non-Christians, including Jews, were wrongly blamed for causing the disease, and some were attacked or forced into separate parts of towns. The economy also changed. With fewer peasants, agricultural land was left unused and livestock died because no one cared for them. Food supplies fell, prices rose, and surviving peasants and craftsmen could demand higher wages because workers had become scarce.

questions.question: How much did England’s population fall by?
questions.modelAnswer: About one-third.

questions.question: What happened to many villages?
questions.modelAnswer: Many were wiped out or abandoned.

questions.question: Why did food prices rise?
questions.modelAnswer: There were fewer workers, so less food was produced and supplies ran low.

questions.question: Why could surviving workers demand higher wages?
questions.modelAnswer: Labour was scarce, so lords and employers had to compete for workers.

questions.question: What does the persecution of Jews show about fear during the plague?
questions.modelAnswer: It shows that fear and ignorance encouraged people to blame vulnerable minority groups.

data.sourcePassage: The effects of the Black Death lasted long after 1350. Many villages disappeared because their populations became too small to survive. Some were only rediscovered centuries later through aerial photography. The plague returned roughly every ten years, so the population of England continued to decline over the following century. Religion was also affected. Many people lost faith in the Christian Church because they had been told the disease was a punishment from God, yet priests, monks and people who prayed also died. The government tried to control wages through the Ordinance of Labourers in 1349, but many workers ignored it. Long-term anger over labour duties, wages and taxation helped create tensions that contributed to the Peasants’ Revolt of 1381.
data.targetPassage: The effects of the Black Death lasted long after 1350. Many villages disappeared because their populations became too small to survive. Some were only rediscovered centuries later through aerial photography. The plague returned roughly every ten years, so the population of England continued to decline over the following century. Religion was also affected. Many people lost faith in the Christian Church because they had been told the disease was a punishment from God, yet priests, monks and people who prayed also died. The government tried to control wages through the Ordinance of Labourers in 1349, but many workers ignored it. Long-term anger over labour duties, wages and taxation helped create tensions that contributed to the Peasants’ Revolt of 1381.

questions.question: How often did the plague return after the first outbreak?
questions.modelAnswer: Roughly every ten years.

questions.question: What law tried to keep wages low in 1349?
questions.modelAnswer: The Ordinance of Labourers.

questions.question: Why did some people lose faith in the Church?
questions.modelAnswer: Priests, monks and people who prayed still died, which weakened trust in religious explanations.

questions.question: How did the Black Death contribute to the Peasants’ Revolt?
questions.modelAnswer: It increased worker confidence and tensions over wages, labour duties and taxation.

questions.question: Which rebellion took place in 1381?
questions.modelAnswer: The Peasants’ Revolt took place in 1381.
